content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 12, 2016

Charles Stone
Belmont City Councilmember
1025 Alameda de las Pulgas, #224
Belmont, CA 94002

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-013

Dear Mr. Stone:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding your duties as a Belmont City
Councilmember under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Because the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of
fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), this letter is based on the facts
presented. Please note that we only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as Section 1090 or common law
conflict of interest.

QUESTION

Would the Act prohibit you from taking part in the governmental decision on whether to
approve the Belmont Village Specific Plan ("specific plan") if you accept an offer of an "of counsel"
position with a law firm that handles slip and fall defense cases for a prominent grocery
store chain with a store within the area of the specific plan?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act would not prohibit you from taking part in the decision on whether to approve
the specific plan because the decision would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on your sources of income.

_________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-013
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are a councilmember for the City of Belmont and an attorney with a solo practice. You
have recently been offered an "of counsel" position with a law firm that handles slip and fall
defense cases for Safeway, a prominent grocery store chain with stores throughout the San
Francisco Bay Area.

You have no ownership in the law firm and would not acquire any as a result of accepting
the position. The firm would pay you as an independent contractor for hours worked on its behalf.
Approximately half your time would be spent on cases for Safeway, with the other half spent on
other matters. The firm would decide whether to bill Safeway or other clients for your time as it
sees fit. Your work on behalf of Safeway would be limited solely to slip and fall work. Based on the
firm's past experience, you expect that you will earn more than $500 in income from cases
involving Safeway in any 12-month period if you accept the position.

Safeway has over 1,300 stores in 19 states, and its parent company is one of the largest food
and drug retailers in the United States, with over 2,200 stores in 33 states and the District of
Columbia. The parent company employs approximately 265,000 people. Safeway operates a large
grocery store at the intersection of Emmet Avenue and Sixth Avenue in downtown Belmont within
the specific plan area. Safeway owns the real property underlying the store.

The specific plan area's current land uses and regulations date back to 1990, when the City
adopted its first Downtown Specific Plan, and an update is necessary to address the area's
contemporary challenges and to foster the community's current vision for the area. The specific
plan will, among other things, affect acceptable land uses within the area; determine the location
and intensity of public and private transportation, sewage, water, drainage, solid waste disposal,
energy, and other essential facilities to be located within the area; set forth the standards and criteria
for future development within the area; and develop a program of implementation measures to
accomplish these objectives.

The City is currently in the process of preparing the specific plan for the City Council's
consideration, and the decision on whether to approve the specific plan is tentatively scheduled to
come before the City Council in December of 2016.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using his or
her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's interests. Section 87103 also sets forth the interests from which a conflict of interest
may arise under the Act.

File No. A-16-013
Page No. 3

Sources of Income:

Based on the information you provided, the only financial interest implicated by the decision
on whether to recommend approval of the specific plan is your interest in any source of income
aggregating $500 or more promised to or received by you within 12 months prior to the decision.

If you accept the "of counsel" position with the law firm, you will be paid an hourly rate by the
firm for the hours you work on its behalf. You expect to earn more than $500 in income from cases
involving Safeway in any 12-month period. Therefore, if you accept the position, you will have a
financial interest in the firm as a source of income once $500 in compensation is promised to or
received by you from the firm within the 12 months preceding the decision.

Also at issue is whether you will have a financial interest in Safeway as a source of income.
Under some circumstances, the Commission will attribute income to more than one source, and will
"pierce" through a business entity that is a source of income and also attribute the public official's income to
the client of the employer (i.e., the customer of the business). (Dorsey Advice Letter, No. A-87-176.) In
that case, an official was employed by a personnel services firm as a temporary assignment employee,
the firm placed the official on a temporary assignment with a real estate company that was a client of
the firm, and the client had business before the official's agency requiring its approval.² We concluded
that the client was also a source of income to the official based on the income the official received from
the client through the firm. (Ibid.)

In the matter at hand, the law firm initiated the potential economic relationship between you and
Safeway when it offered you the "of counsel" position. If you accept the position, the firm would have
the authority to terminate the relationship for any reason, but it would not be able to extend it without
Safeway's consent. Safeway would have the authority to continue or to terminate the relationship for any
reason; if Safeway was unsatisfied with you or the firm, it could unilaterally cease doing business with
either you or the firm or both; if Safeway wished to continue to employ you against the wishes of the
firm, it could contract with you directly. In effect, Safeway would have sole authority over the
continuation of its relationship with you. Therefore, we find that you will also have a financial interest
in Safeway as a source of income if you accept the position and receive the requisite compensation
within the specified time.

Foreseeability:

In order to determine if you have a conflict of interest under the Act with respect to the decision
on whether to approve the specific plan, we must consider whether a financial effect of the decision on
one or more of your interests would be reasonably foreseeable. Under the Act, an effect on an interest is
presumed foreseeable if the interest is explicitly involved in the decision, and an interest is "explicitly
involved" if the interest is a named party in, or subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).)

In this case, your interests are not explicitly involved in the decision on whether to approve the
specific plan. Therefore, an effect of the decision on one or more of your interests is reasonably

² In Dorsey, we took into account the following information: (1) the personal services firm initiated the
economic relationship between the client and the official; (2) the firm had the authority to terminate the relationship but
not to extend it without the consent of the client; (3) the client had the authority to continue or to terminate the
relationship for any reason; and (4) the client had sole authority to extend the relationship beyond the original
contract for services. Therefore, we concluded that the client had control over the continuation of its contractual
relationship with the official.

File No. A-16-013
Page No. 4

foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical.
(Regulation 18701(b).)

If approved, the specific plan would affect land uses, determine the location and intensity of
essential facilities, and set standards and criteria for future development within the specific plan
area. Although the completion of these tasks may result in the need for additional legal work from
the City or businesses with interests within the specific plan area, there is no indication that this
particular law firm will obtain more legal work as a result of the approval of the specific plan.
Therefore, based on the facts presented, we find no reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the
decision on the firm.

Safeway operates a large grocery store within the specific plan area, and owns the real
property underlying the store. As noted above, if approved, the specific plan would affect land uses,
and determine the location and intensity of essential facilities, within the specific plan area. A
liberalization of authorized land uses within the specific plan area might allow Safeway to pursue
an expansion of its store, and the determination of the location and intensity of essential facilities
could affect the amount of business that store generates. Accordingly, we find that the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Safeway.

Materiality:

Regulation 18702.1(b) provides the test for whether an effect on an official's interest in a
business entity (including a business entity that is a source of income) is material.³ Regulation
18702.1(b) provides in pertinent part as follows:

"[The] financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information
would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would
contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock,
or the value of a privately-held business."

Safeway has over 1,300 stores in 19 states, and its parent company is one of the largest food
and drug retailers in the United States, with over 2,200 stores in 33 states and the District of
Columbia. The parent company employs approximately 265,000 people. Given the size of Safeway,
the effect of the decision would not contribute to a change in its value. Therefore, we find that the
decision would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your interest in
Safeway as a source of income.

· Accordingly, we conclude that even if you accept the "of counsel" position and the grocery
store chain becomes a sufficient source of income prior to the decision on whether to approve the
specific plan, the Act would not prohibit you from participating in the decision.

³ Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the standard for materiality applicable to the effect of a decision on an official's
interest in a source of income. Regulation 18702.3(a)(4) provides that if the source of income is a business entity,
materiality is instead determined pursuant to the standard for materiality for the effect of a decision on an official's
interest in a business entity contained in Regulation 18702.1.

File No. A-16-013
Page No. 5

You have also asked, if Safeway submits an application for a project to the City at some point in the future, whether the Act would prohibit you from taking part in the decision on that application. Regulation 18702.1(a)(1) provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a decision on a business entity in which an official is financially interested due to that business entity being a source of income is material if the business entity initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or request for other government action concerning the business entity. Therefore, if Safeway submits an application for a project to the City, and if Safeway is a source of income to you under the Act, the Act would prohibit you from taking part in that decision because that decision would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your financial interest in Safeway. However, because we lack the facts necessary to conduct a complete analysis of this hypothetical question, we suggest that you seek further advice if that situation arises.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

store chain with a store within the area of the specific plan?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act would not prohibit you from taking part in the decision on whether to approve 
the specific plan because the decision would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial 
effect on your sources of income.

QUESTION: Would the Act prohibit you from taking part in the governmental decision on whether to 
approve the Belmont Village Specific Plan ("specific plan") if you accept an offer of an "of 
counsel" position with a law firm that handles slip and fall defense cases for a prominent grocery 
store chain with a store within the area of the specific plan?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act would not prohibit you from taking part in the decision on whether to approve 
the specific plan because the decision would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial 
effect on your sources of income.
